name: Kangla Sha — Guardian of Kangla - Unisex oversized fit with drop shoulders
desc: A heritage streetwear tee inspired by Kangla Sha, the sacred guardian linked to Kangla Fort in Imphal. The design keeps the front minimal and the back bold, turning a powerful Meitei cultural symbol into a clean everyday statement piece. Kangla Fort is described by Incredible India as the ancient capital of Manipur and a place of historical and religious importance, while Kangla Sha is presented there as the mythical guardian of Kangla and protector of the Kangla Uttra.
lore: Kangla Sha is one of the strongest symbols connected to Kangla Fort, the historic seat of Manipur’s royal power. Official travel material says the original Kangla Sha statues were guardians of Kangla with a dragon’s head and lion’s body, stationed at the coronation hall. The figure is also treated as a state-level emblem of Manipur in government records. A historical account published by Penguin notes that the pair was first constructed by Maharaja Chourjit in 1804 and later rebuilt by Maharaja Narasingh in 1844. This design is inspired by guardianship, royal heritage, ancestral memory, and the quiet strength of Meitei identity. The idea is to carry Kangla Sha not as decoration alone, but as a living symbol of protection, dignity, and rooted pride. Kangla itself is described as the ancient capital and a sacred, historic center for the Meitei people.
details: 90/10 cotton polyester, 260 GSM Terry-knit texture with a premium hand-feel
wash: Cold wash only, do not iron print

name: Keibu Keioiba — Tiger-Man of Manipur - Unisex oversized fit with drop shoulders
desc: A heritage streetwear piece inspired by one of the most iconic heroic moments from the Khamba-Thoibi epic. The design captures the raw confrontation between man and beast, turning a legendary act of courage into a bold modern visual. Khamba Na Kao Phaba refers to the legendary feat of Khuman Khamba capturing the divine wild bull known as “Kao” in Meitei folklore. The Kao is described as a powerful, almost supernatural bull from the Moirang kingdom. In the epic, Khamba is challenged to capture it as part of a trial set against him. Instead of brute force alone, Khamba tames the beast by recognizing its past connection to his family and calming it, showing both strength and wisdom. This event is one of the defining heroic acts in the Khamba-Thoibi epic, a central narrative of Meitei mythology dating back to the ancient Moirang kingdom. Fearless courage, control over chaos, strength guided by intelligence, and the idea that true power is not just force but understanding. The design reflects the moment where man faces something greater than himself and wins not by domination, but by connection and inner strength.
lore: Keibu Keioiba is a famous Manipuri folktale character from Meitei folklore, often described as a tiger-man or a half-man, half-tiger being. A widely told version says he was once Kabui Salang Maiba, a skilled priest who used magic to transform into a tiger and could not fully return to human form. The tale is part of Manipur’s phunga wari, or fireside storytelling tradition.
details: 90/10 cotton polyester, 260 GSM Terry-knit texture with a premium hand-feel
wash: Cold wash only, do not iron print

name: Khamba Na Kao Phaba — Taming the Divine - Unisex oversized fit with drop shoulders
desc: A conceptual streetwear piece that reimagines the legendary Khamba and Kao encounter through a dual-state visual. The design contrasts the wild, untamed force of the divine bull with its calm, controlled form, symbolizing transformation through strength and understanding. In Meitei folklore, Khamba Na Kao Phaba is the legendary act where Khuman Khamba captures the divine bull “Kao” of Moirang. The Kao is described as a powerful and almost supernatural being. Instead of defeating it through brute force alone, Khamba is said to have recognized its connection to his lineage and used a rope associated with his father to calm and control the beast. This act demonstrated not just physical strength, but wisdom, identity, and rightful authority, making it one of the defining moments of the Khamba–Thoibi epic. Duality of power — chaos vs control, beast vs balance. Inspired by the moment of transformation where violence turns into harmony. The split visual reflects both the untamed spirit of the Kao and its eventual submission, blended with anime-style intensity to make the story visually modern while staying rooted in tradition.
lore: In Meitei folklore, Khamba Na Kao Phaba is the legendary act where Khuman Khamba captures the divine bull “Kao” of Moirang. The Kao is described as a powerful and almost supernatural being. Instead of defeating it through brute force alone, Khamba is said to have recognized its connection to his lineage and used a rope associated with his father to calm and control the beast. This act demonstrated not just physical strength, but wisdom, identity, and rightful authority, making it one of the defining moments of the Khamba–Thoibi epic.
details: 90/10 cotton polyester, 260 GSM Terry-knit texture with a premium hand-feel
wash: Cold wash only, do not iron print

name: Lai Khushangbi — Whispers of the Night - Unisex oversized fit with drop shoulders
desc: A dark folklore-inspired streetwear piece rooted in Manipuri fireside storytelling culture. The design captures the eerie presence of Hingchabi (Hingchani) in shadow form, paired with the intimate moment of a grandmother narrating the tale, blending fear, memory, and tradition into a single visual. Childhood memory, fear shaped by storytelling, generational connection, and the power of oral tradition. Inspired by moments of sitting with elders, listening to stories that blurred imagination and reality. The shadowy figure represents how these stories lived in the mind — unseen, but deeply felt.
lore: Lai Khushangbi is part of the oral storytelling tradition of Manipur, commonly shared through phunga wari (fireside stories) passed down by elders. The tale often includes the presence of Hingchabi (also referred to as Hingchani), a mysterious and feared female spirit figure associated with darkness and cautionary storytelling. These stories were traditionally told to children as both entertainment and moral guidance, embedding cultural memory through generations rather than formal written texts.
details: 90/10 cotton polyester, 260 GSM Terry-knit texture with a premium hand-feel
wash: Cold wash only, do not iron print

name: Lai Khushangbi — The Faceless Reach
desc: A minimal yet striking streetwear piece centered around a single defining element—the unnatural reach. The design isolates the elongated arm of Lai Khushangbi and pairs it with a bold statement, turning folklore into a clean, modern visual warning. Lai Khushangbi is part of Manipur’s oral storytelling tradition, passed through phunga wari (fireside stories). The figure is often associated with fear, darkness, and cautionary presence, commonly linked with Hingchabi (Hingchani), a mysterious feminine spirit. Rather than fixed descriptions, the fear comes from suggestion—an unseen entity that does not announce itself but is suddenly there, shaped by imagination across generations. Focused on one idea: presence without warning. Inspired by childhood fear where danger wasn’t seen approaching—it was already there. The elongated arm becomes the identity of the entity, symbolizing reach beyond distance, beyond safety. The line “She doesn’t knock, she reaches” turns folklore into a modern, memorable statement.
lore: Lai Khushangbi is part of Manipur’s oral storytelling tradition, passed through phunga wari (fireside stories). The figure is often associated with fear, darkness, and cautionary presence, commonly linked with Hingchabi (Hingchani), a mysterious feminine spirit. Rather than fixed descriptions, the fear comes from suggestion—an unseen entity that does not announce itself but is suddenly there, shaped by imagination across generations.
details: 90/10 cotton polyester, 260 GSM Terry-knit texture with a premium hand-feel
wash: Cold wash only, do not iron print

name: Lai Khushangbi — She Reaches - Unisex oversized fit with drop shoulders
desc: A minimalist yet haunting streetwear piece that focuses on the raw visual identity of Lai Khushangbi. Stripped down to a sketch-style composition, the design emphasizes elongated arms and an undefined face, creating a powerful silhouette rooted in fear and imagination. First concept of Project Eramdam — focusing on form over detail. Inspired by the idea that fear comes from what is not fully seen. The long arms represent unnatural reach, while the faceless design reflects how these stories lived in memory—unclear, distorted, but deeply real. The sketch style keeps it raw, like something drawn from memory rather than reality.
lore: Lai Khushangbi exists within the oral storytelling tradition of Manipur, shared through phunga wari (fireside storytelling). The figure is often associated with eerie presence, darkness, and cautionary tales, with Hingchabi (Hingchani) representing a feared feminine spirit form. Descriptions vary across storytellers, but the fear is consistent—an unseen presence more felt than clearly seen, shaped by imagination rather than fixed depiction.
details: 90/10 cotton polyester, 260 GSM Terry-knit texture with a premium hand-feel
wash: Cold wash only, do not iron print

name: Sangai — The Dancing Deer of Loktak - Unisex oversized fit with drop shoulders
desc: A cultural streetwear piece celebrating the Sangai, Manipur’s iconic deer known for its graceful, dancing gait. The design blends elegance and strength, turning a symbol of identity and pride into a bold wearable statement. Pride, identity, and graceful strength. Inspired by the “dancing deer” concept—movement that appears delicate but is deeply adapted and resilient. The design reflects cultural pride, natural balance, and the uniqueness of Manipur’s ecosystem, blending traditional symbolism with bold modern typography.
lore: The Sangai (Rucervus eldii eldii), also known as the brow-antlered deer, is the state animal of Manipur. It is uniquely found in the floating phumdis of Keibul Lamjao National Park on Loktak Lake, the world’s only floating national park. Its distinctive slow and delicate movement on the floating biomass gives it the name “dancing deer,” making it a symbol of harmony between nature and life in Manipur.
details: 90/10 cotton polyester, 260 GSM Terry-knit texture with a premium hand-feel
wash: Cold wash only, do not iron print

name: Sangai — Guardian of Loktak - Unisex oversized fit with drop shoulders
desc: A bold reinterpretation of the Sangai as a spiritual guardian. The design blends realism with symbolic energy, presenting the deer not just as an animal, but as a silent protector of land, culture, and identity. Protection, identity, and continuity of culture. Inspired by the idea that nature itself watches and preserves heritage. The glowing and intensified form represents the Sangai as a spirit-like presence—quiet, observant, and enduring—bridging tradition with modern visual storytelling.
lore: The Sangai (Rucervus eldii eldii) is the state animal of Manipur and is native to the floating phumdis of Keibul Lamjao National Park on Loktak Lake. Known as the “dancing deer” due to its delicate movement, it represents harmony with nature. While traditionally understood as a real and endangered species, this design presents an artistic interpretation of the Sangai as a symbolic guardian watching over the land and its people.
details: 90/10 cotton polyester, 260 GSM Terry-knit texture with a premium hand-feel
wash: Cold wash only, do not iron print

name: Thangal General — Honor Before Surrender
desc: A tribute streetwear piece honoring Thangal General, one of Manipur’s most respected historical figures. The design reflects sacrifice, courage, and unwavering loyalty, turning history into a powerful modern statement. Honor, resistance, and sacrifice. Inspired by the idea of standing firm even in defeat. The design reflects a warrior who chose dignity over surrender, representing the pride and resilience of Manipur’s past and its people.
lore: Thangal General (Major General Thangal), also known as Thangal Major, was a key military leader of the Kingdom of Manipur. He played a crucial role during the Anglo-Manipur War of 1891, standing alongside Manipuri forces against British colonial rule. After the conflict, he was captured and later executed by the British in 1891. He is remembered as a symbol of resistance, bravery, and sacrifice in Manipuri history.
details: 90/10 cotton polyester, 260 GSM Terry-knit texture with a premium hand-feel
wash: Cold wash only, do not iron print

name: Uchek Langmeidong — The Free Hornbill - Unisex oversized fit with drop shoulders
desc: A folklore streetwear piece inspired by the Manipuri hornbill tale, showing the moment of transformation from pain into liberation. The design reflects a girl becoming a bird, not as escape alone, but as the birth of freedom. The story is centered on the hornbill, Uchek Langmei, and on transformation through suffering. Freedom, endurance, survival, and rising beyond cruelty. Inspired by the idea of becoming something untouchable and unbroken. The bird is not only a symbol of escape, but of elevation—she leaves as a free spirit, no longer trapped by human suffering.
lore: Uchek Langmeidong, also rendered in E-Pao as “Human turning into Uchek Langmei / Meena Uchek Langmei Onba,” is a Manipuri folktale from the oral storytelling collection *Fungawari Singbul* compiled by B. Jayantakumar Sharma. In one widely shared version, Nongdangnu is mistreated by her stepmother, asks hornbills for feathers, transforms into a bird, and flies away, telling her father she can no longer bear the cruelty and must live free.
details: 90/10 cotton polyester, 260 GSM Terry-knit texture with a premium hand-feel
wash: Cold wash only, do not iron print
